epigraph.text: He has inscribed a circle on the face of the waters at the boundary between light and darkness.

content.text:
The sphere is perhaps the most perfect of all solid figures. Every 
point on its surface is equidistant from its center—a three-dimensional 
analog of the circle's perfect symmetry. We find spheres throughout 
creation: the sun and moon, water droplets, soap bubbles, and the 
earth itself. This ubiquity arises from a remarkable property: the 
sphere encloses the maximum volume for a given surface area, making 
it nature's preferred shape for minimizing surface tension and 
gravitational energy.

The formulas for the surface area and volume of a sphere were 
discovered by Archimedes of Syracuse (287–212 BCE), who considered 
these discoveries among his greatest achievements. According to 
legend, he requested that a sphere inscribed in a cylinder be 
engraved on his tombstone, commemorating his proof that the sphere 
has exactly two-thirds the volume and surface area of its 
circumscribing cylinder.

content.title: Sphere
content.content:
A **sphere** is the set of all points in 
three-dimensional space that are equidistant from a fixed point 
called the **center**. The common 
distance from the center to any point on the sphere is called 
the **radius**.

content.text:
Just as a circle divides a plane into interior and exterior regions, 
a sphere divides space into an interior (all points closer to the 
center than $r$), the sphere itself (all points exactly distance $r$ 
from the center), and an exterior (all points farther than $r$).

content.title: Parts of a Sphere
content.content:
Let $S$ be a sphere with center $O$ and radius $r$.

- A **diameter** is a chord 
 that passes through the center. Its length is $d = 2r$.

- A **great circle** is the 
 intersection of the sphere with a plane passing through the 
 center. Every great circle has radius $r$ (the same as the 
 sphere) and divides the sphere into two equal hemispheres.

- A **hemisphere** is half of a 
 sphere, cut by a great circle.

- A **small circle** is the intersection of the 
 sphere with a plane that does *not* pass through the 
 center. Small circles have radius less than $r$.

content.title: Finding small circle radius
content.solution:
The cross-section shows a right triangle with the sphere's radius 
as hypotenuse, the distance from center to plane as one leg, and 
the small circle's radius as the other leg.

Using the Pythagorean Theorem:
\[
r^2 + 6^2 = 10^2
\]
\[
r^2 = 100 - 36 = 64
\]
\[
r = 8
\]

The small circle has radius 8.

content.text:
Archimedes proved that the surface area of a sphere equals the 
lateral surface area of its circumscribing cylinder—the cylinder 
with the same radius and height equal to the diameter of the sphere.

The lateral area of the cylinder is $2\pi r \cdot 2r = 4\pi r^2$. 
Archimedes showed this equals the sphere's surface area.

content.title: Surface Area of a Sphere
content.content:
The surface area of a sphere with radius $r$ is:
\[
S = 4\pi r^2
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Surface area of a sphere
content.problem: Find the surface area of a sphere with radius 7 cm.

content.title: Finding radius from surface area
content.problem:
A basketball has a surface area of approximately $1810$ cm$^2$. 
Find its radius.
content.solution:
Using $S = 4\pi r^2$:
$$
1810 &= 4\pi r^2 \\
r^2 &= \frac{1810}{4\pi} \approx 144.03 \\
r &\approx 12 \text{ cm}
$$

The basketball has radius approximately 12 cm (diameter 24 cm, 
or about 9.4 inches).

content.title: Surface area of Earth
content.problem:
The Earth's radius is approximately 6371 km. Estimate Earth's 
surface area.
content.solution:
\[
S = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi(6371)^2 = 4\pi(40{,}589{,}641) \approx 510{,}000{,}000 \text{ km}^2
\]

Earth's surface area is approximately 510 million km$^2$.

content.title: Surface area of a hemisphere
content.problem:
Find the total surface area of a hemisphere (half-sphere) with 
radius 5, including the circular base.
content.solution:
The hemisphere consists of:

- Half of the sphere's curved surface: $\frac{1}{2}(4\pi r^2) = 2\pi r^2$

- The circular base: $\pi r^2$

Total surface area:
\[
S = 2\pi r^2 + \pi r^2 = 3\pi r^2 = 3\pi(25) = 75\pi \approx 235.6
\]

content.text:
Archimedes proved that the volume of a sphere is exactly two-thirds 
the volume of its circumscribing cylinder. The cylinder has volume 
$\pi r^2 \cdot 2r = 2\pi r^3$, so the sphere has volume 
$\frac{2}{3}(2\pi r^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.

content.title: Volume of a Sphere
content.content:
The volume of a sphere with radius $r$ is:
\[
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Archimedes proved this using the "method of exhaustion"—a 
precursor to integral calculus. He showed that the sphere can be 
approximated by stacking thin circular disks of varying radii.

content.title: Volume of a sphere
content.problem: Find the volume of a sphere with radius 6.

content.title: Finding radius from volume
content.problem:
A spherical balloon has a volume of $36\pi$ cubic inches. Find 
its radius.

content.title: Volume of Earth
content.problem: Estimate the volume of Earth (radius $\approx 6371$ km).
content.solution:
\[
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi(6371)^3 \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi(2.586 \times 10^{11})
\]
\[
V \approx 1.083 \times 10^{12} \text{ km}^3
\]

Earth's volume is approximately 1.083 trillion cubic kilometers.

content.title: Volume of a hemisphere
content.problem: A hemispherical bowl has radius 12 cm. How much water can it hold?
content.solution:
The volume of a hemisphere is half the volume of a full sphere:
\[
V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3}\pi(1728) = 1152\pi \approx 3619 \text{ cm}^3
\]

This equals approximately 3.619 liters.

content.text:
Archimedes considered his discovery about the sphere and its 
circumscribing cylinder to be his greatest achievement. The 
relationship is beautifully simple:

content.title: Archimedes' Theorem
content.content:
For a sphere inscribed in a cylinder (where the cylinder has the 
same radius as the sphere and height equal to the sphere's diameter):

1. $\displaystyle \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cylinder}}} = \frac{2}{3}$

2. $\displaystyle \frac{S_{\text{sphere}}}{S_{\text{cylinder (lateral)}}} = 1$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let the sphere have radius $r$. The cylinder has radius $r$ and 
height $2r$.

**Volume comparison:**
$$
V_{\text{sphere}} &= \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \\
V_{\text{cylinder}} &= \pi r^2(2r) = 2\pi r^3 \\
\frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cylinder}}} &= \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{2\pi r^3} = \frac{4/3}{2} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

**Surface area comparison:**
$$
S_{\text{sphere}} &= 4\pi r^2 \\
S_{\text{cylinder (lateral)}} &= 2\pi r(2r) = 4\pi r^2 \\
\frac{S_{\text{sphere}}}{S_{\text{cylinder (lateral)}}} &= \frac{4\pi r^2}{4\pi r^2} = 1 \qedhere
$$

content.title: Verifying Archimedes' theorem
content.problem:
A sphere with radius 5 is inscribed in a cylinder. Find the 
volumes of both and verify that their ratio is $\frac{2}{3}$.
content.solution:
The sphere has volume:
\[
V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi(125) = \frac{500\pi}{3}
\]

The cylinder has radius 5 and height 10, so:
\[
V_{\text{cylinder}} = \pi(25)(10) = 250\pi
\]

The ratio is:
\[
\frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cylinder}}} = \frac{500\pi/3}{250\pi} = \frac{500}{750} = \frac{2}{3} \checkmark
\]

content.text: When a plane cuts through a sphere, it creates interesting regions:

content.title: Spherical Cap and Zone
content.content:
A **spherical cap** (or **dome**) 
is the portion of a sphere cut off by a single plane.

A **spherical zone** is the portion 
of a sphere between two parallel planes.

content.title: Surface Area of a Spherical Zone
content.content:
The surface area of a spherical zone (the curved surface between 
two parallel planes) depends only on the sphere's radius $r$ and 
the height $h$ of the zone (the distance between the planes):
\[
S_{\text{zone}} = 2\pi rh
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This remarkable result means that zones of equal height on a 
sphere have equal surface areas, regardless of where they are 
located on the sphere!

content.title: Area of a spherical cap
content.problem:
A sphere has radius 10. A plane cuts through the sphere 3 units 
from the top. Find the surface area of the spherical cap.
content.solution:
The height of the cap is $h = 3$.

Using the zone formula:
\[
S = 2\pi rh = 2\pi(10)(3) = 60\pi
\]

marginNotes.text:
It is he who sits above the circle 
of the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers.

[The Lord] covering yourself with 
light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent.

Who determined its measurements—surely 
you know! Or who stretched the line upon it?

He has made everything beautiful 
in its time. Also, he has put eternity into man's heart.
